purpose: Organizing macro scenarios for dollar direction: risk-off strength (left), weak-growth weakness (middle), strong-growth strength (right).
components: Left tail: risk-off dollar strength (potentially impaired by fiscal dominance)
Middle zone: weak-growth dollar weakness (potentially supported by fiscal expansion / asymmetric smirk)
Right tail: strong-growth dollar strength (potentially capped by growth convergence)
Recalibrated range: DXY 95-108 (critiqued as not evidence-derived)
Risk-off regime: dollar strengthens during global risk events due to safe-haven demand
Risk-on regime: dollar weakens as capital flows to higher-yielding and growth assets globally
US exceptionalism regime: dollar strengthens when US growth outperforms relative to the rest of the world
Fiscal smirk modification: adds fiscal deficit dynamics as a fourth dimension that can reinforce or undermine any of the three regimes
current_assessment: Descriptively useful but unfalsifiable — three collectively exhaustive regimes mean any outcome can be explained ex post. No documented ex-ante regime identification rules with hit rates exist. The fiscal smirk modification adds another degree of freedom. The framework persists because Meese-Rogoff (1983) shows random walk beats all FX models at short horizons, leaving an analytical void.
limitations: No real-time regime identification rule with documented hit rates produced by any agent
Three regimes exhaustively cover all macro states, making the framework unfalsifiable
Historical counterexamples demonstrate predictive failure in ~20% of year-periods
Recalibrations (e.g., DXY 95-108) not derived from the analytical framework itself
Cannot handle fiscal dominance left-tail bifurcation without ad hoc modification
Collectively exhaustive regimes make the framework unfalsifiable
No ex-ante regime identification rule with documented hit rates
Historical counterexamples (2017-18, Q2-Q3 2020) dismissed as transitional rather than framework failures
Fiscal smirk adds another degree of freedom, further reducing falsifiability
Any invocation should require stated ex-ante regime calls with documented hit rates
